did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The study used a convolutional neural network (CNN) based on a pretrained ResNet-50 architecture to perform binary classification of high- versus low-risk histopathologic subtypes of basal cell carcinoma (BCC) using dermoscopic images. The AI model's performance (AUC 0.75) was evaluated and compared against dermatologist evaluations.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The document is explicitly labeled as an 'ORIGINAL ARTICLE' and describes a retrospective single-center study involving original data collection (1580 dermoscopic images), model development (CNN training), and experimental evaluation (comparison with dermatologists). It presents new results, including AUC metrics and statistical associations, rather than summarizing prior literature.

title: Assessment of high- and low-risk histopathologic subtypes of basal cell carcinoma using artificial intelligence and dermatologist evaluation
brief_summary: This retrospective single-center study evaluated the diagnostic performance of a convolutional neural network (CNN) for binary classification of high- versus low-risk histopathologic subtypes of basal cell carcinoma (BCC) using dermoscopic images. The CNN, based on a pretrained ResNet-50 architecture, was trained on 1580 dermoscopic images and tested on a separate set of 249 images. Its performance was compared against the assessments of nine board-certified dermatologists who evaluated the same test set. The CNN achieved an area under the receiver operating characteristic curve (AUC) of 0.75, which was comparable to the combined dermatologist group's AUC of 0.79 (P = .18). The study concludes that CNNs show promise as a useful tool for assisting in the histopathologic risk stratification of BCC.

lesion_types_studied.details: The study focused exclusively on histopathologically confirmed basal cell carcinoma tumors. The experiment involved binary classification of BCC into high-risk and low-risk subtypes.

lesion_types_studied.lesion_type: High-risk histopathologic subtype of basal cell carcinoma
lesion_types_studied.role: Target class (positive class in binary classification)
lesion_types_studied.details: One of the two classes in the binary classification task. Included micronodular, infiltrative, morpheaform, and basosquamous variants. Counted 828 cases in the combined training/validation set and 131 in the test set.

lesion_types_studied.lesion_type: Low-risk histopathologic subtype of basal cell carcinoma
lesion_types_studied.role: Target class (negative class in binary classification)
lesion_types_studied.details: One of the two classes in the binary classification task. Included nodular and superficial variants. Counted 752 cases in the combined training/validation set and 118 in the test set.

all_task_types.target: Binary classification of high-risk versus low-risk histopathologic subtypes of basal cell carcinoma (BCC)
all_task_types.details: The study performs binary classification to distinguish between high-risk BCC (HR-BCC, including micronodular, infiltrative, morpheaform, and basosquamous variants) and low-risk BCC (LR-BCC, including nodular and superficial variants) using dermoscopic images. The model predicts discrete diagnostic categories (HR-BCC vs LR-BCC). Performance was evaluated using the Area Under the Receiver Operating Characteristic Curve (AUC ROC), with HR-BCC serving as the positive class.

datasets_used.name: Sahlgrenska University Hospital BCC Cohort
datasets_used.description: A retrospective single-center cohort of histopathologically verified basal cell carcinoma (BCC) tumors diagnosed between 2016 and 2022 at Sahlgrenska University Hospital, Gothenburg, Sweden. Data included dermoscopic and clinical images linked to pathology reports. Images were acquired using DermLite DL3/DL4 devices and iPhone 8 Plus smartphones. The dataset is available via Mendeley (https://data.mendeley.com/datasets/n8bfdwkhy6/1).
classes_used: High-risk histopathologic subtype of basal cell carcinoma (HR-BCC)
Low-risk histopathologic subtype of basal cell carcinoma (LR-BCC)

use.training: Used to train a convolutional neural network (CNN) based on a pretrained ResNet-50 architecture. The combined training and validation set consisted of 1331 images (697 HR-BCC, 634 LR-BCC).
use.validation: Included within the combined training and validation set (1331 images) for model development and hyperparameter tuning via cross-validation.
use.testing: Used to evaluate the diagnostic performance of the CNN and compare it against 9 board-certified dermatologists. The test set consisted of 249 images (131 HR-BCC, 118 LR-BCC).
use.other: Used for univariable logistic regression analysis to assess associations between clinical/dermoscopic features and histopathologic subtype.

metrics_used_to_evaluate_results: Area under the receiver operating characteristic curve (AUC ROC)

statistical_methods_used.purpose: To assess statistical differences between AUC ROC values of the CNN models and the combined dermatologist group.
To assess associations between predefined clinical and dermoscopic features and histopathologic risk category (HR-BCC vs LR-BCC), estimating odds ratios.
To provide uncertainty estimation for AUC ROC values and Odds Ratios (ORs).
To assess interobserver variability (agreement) among dermatologists in the evaluation of predefined clinical and dermoscopic features.
To summarize patient demographics, anatomic tumor locations, and the distribution of LR-BCC and HR-BCC across the datasets.

hypothesis_testing.null_hypothesis: There is no significant difference between the area under the receiver operating characteristic curve (AUC ROC) of the CNN models and the combined dermatologist group.
hypothesis_testing.alternative_hypothesis: There is a significant difference between the AUC ROC of the CNN models and the combined dermatologist group.
hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The difference in AUC ROC between the CNN (0.75) and the combined dermatologist group (0.79) was not statistically significant (P = .18), indicating comparable diagnostic performance.

test_set.notes: The CNN performance was comparable to the combined dermatologist group. Adding anatomic location metadata did not improve CNN performance. Individual dermatologist performance is represented by green triangles with 95% CI in Figure 2.

training_and_validation_set.notes: Used for model development. No specific performance metrics reported for this split other than the resulting test set performance.

logistic_regression_results.notes: Depressed surface topography exhibited complete separation (21 HR-BCC, 0 LR-BCC). Reference categories: Raised for surface topography, Absent for all other features.

architecture_used.main_model: Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) based on ResNet-50
architecture_used.input_size: Progressively increased from 254x254 to 448x448 pixels during training

architecture_details.metadata_augmentation: An embedding layer was used to encode anatomic location metadata, which was fused with the image feature representation before the final classification layer

training_details.training_schedule: Images were progressively resized from 254x254 to 448x448 pixels during training
training_details.cross_validation: Used for model development on the combined training and validation set

machine_learning_or_deep_learning_models: Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) based on pretrained ResNet-50 architecture
preprocessing_techniques: Manual quality review to verify diagnostic adequacy
Exclusion of images with insufficient quality, incorrectly labeled tumors, colliding tumors, duplicates, previously biopsied tumors, or non-polarized imaging
Exclusion of cases lacking valid images or specified histopathologic subtype
Automatic trimming of excessive black background using custom Python code
Progressive resizing of images from 254x254 to 448x448 pixels during training
Data augmentation adapted from Bo et al.

data_augmentation_techniques.technique: Data augmentation adapted from Bo et al.
data_augmentation_techniques.details: The paper states that data augmentation was adapted from Bo et al. (reference 16, Ha Q, Liu B, Liu F. Identifying melanoma images using EfficientNet ensemble: winning solution to the SIIM-ISIC melanoma classification challenge), but does not provide specific details on the geometric transforms, color transforms, or probabilities used in this study.

conclusions: Convolutional neural networks (CNNs) appear useful for the binary classification of high- versus low-risk histopathologic subtypes of basal cell carcinoma (BCC).
The diagnostic performance of the CNN (AUC 0.75) was comparable to that of a combined group of nine board-certified dermatologists (AUC 0.79), with no statistically significant difference (P = .18).
Adding anatomic location metadata to the CNN model did not improve its diagnostic performance compared to the image-only model.

Specific clinical and dermoscopic features were significantly associated with high-risk BCC subtypes, including bumpy surface topography, clinical and dermoscopic ulceration, ill-defined borders, focused vessels, and white porcelain areas.
Specific dermoscopic features were significantly associated with low-risk BCC subtypes, including unfocused vessels, erosions, and pigmentation.
important_remarks: The study is a retrospective single-center design, which limits generalizability due to potential differences in image acquisition protocols and dermoscopy devices across other clinics.
There is a population bias as the cohort consisted primarily of Nordic patients with Fitzpatrick skin types 1-3, limiting applicability to populations with darker skin types.

The CNN was trained exclusively on dermoscopic images, whereas the dermatologist comparators had access to both clinical and dermoscopic images, creating an asymmetric information input for the comparison.
Interobserver variability among dermatologists was moderate for many features, which may affect the reliability of the feature-based associations derived from their assessments.

Histopathologic stratification followed a Swedish tradition; while the high/low-risk distinction aligns with international classifications, pathology assessment practices may vary between healthcare systems.
Adding anatomic location metadata to the CNN model did not improve diagnostic performance (AUC remained 0.75), contrary to some prior studies.
